proposals.title: Reframing the SEM Paradigm: Establishing PLS-SEM as the Robust Default for Predictive Modeling in Marketing and Management Research
proposals.motivation: The dominant narrative in structural equation modeling (SEM) literature, pre-2026, frames covariance-based SEM (CB-SEM) as the gold standard for theory testing, with partial least squares SEM (PLS-SEM) often treated as a 'second-generation' alternative with relaxed assumptions. This perspective is increasingly misaligned with the predictive and exploratory aims of contemporary fields like marketing and information systems, where data often violate CB-SEM's strict requirements (e.g., multivariate normality, large sample sizes, correct model specification). A paradigm shift is needed to better align methodological guidance with practical research goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the typical comparison framework, PLS-SEM can be logically positioned as the robust default method for prediction-oriented research, with CB-SEM constituting a special case applicable only when its stringent statistical assumptions are explicitly met and the primary goal is strict parameter estimation for confirmatory analysis.
proposals.method: The research will employ a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study, building on Henseler's prior work on composite model specification. We will systematically vary data conditions (non-normality, model misspecification, small sample sizes) and research objectives (prediction vs. parameter estimation) to compare the performance of PLS-SEM and CB-SEM. The analysis will utilize established pre-2026 algorithms (e.g., PLS path weighting scheme, maximum likelihood estimation) and robustness metrics (e.g., prediction error, bias in path coefficients). A subsequent empirical demonstration will apply this inverted framework to a brand authenticity dataset, akin to Henseler's recent work on AI-disclosed brand voice.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, evidence-based justification for reframing methodological discourse in favor of PLS-SEM for predictive modeling. It will offer clear, practical guidelines for researchers on when to use each method, moving beyond simplistic 'versus' debates. The proposal strengthens the theoretical foundation of PLS-SEM by formally characterizing it as a robust default, thereby empowering researchers in design, marketing, and composites to choose methods aligned with their goals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate inversion of the established methodological hierarchy, challenging the default status of CB-SEM. While previous comparisons list PLS-SEM's advantages, this work formally redefines the 'default' and 'special case' roles based on alignment with practical research objectives and data realities, not just statistical pedigree.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing Established Constructs: A Formative Measurement Approach to Brand Authenticity and Consumer Attitudes
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in psychology and marketing treats constructs like brand authenticity as latent traits measured reflectively. However, this can be theoretically and empirically restrictive when the construct is a composite of distinct, defining facets that collectively cause the latent variable. Pre-2026, the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites in SEM provides a robust framework to challenge this convention, enabling a more nuanced modeling of how multi-dimensional perceptions form holistic judgments.
proposals.hypothesis: For established psychological constructs like brand authenticity, formative measurement models, where indicators are viewed as causal antecedents, will demonstrate superior explanatory power and theoretical fidelity compared to traditional reflective models in predicting key outcomes such as brand attitude.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-study design using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM), leveraging the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for formative composites. Study 1 will be a conceptual replication and extension of prior brand authenticity research, directly comparing reflective and formative model specifications. Study 2 will use experimental data (e.g., manipulating AI-disclosed brand voice) to test the stability and predictive validity of the formative specification across conditions. Model comparisons will be based on established criteria: explanatory power (R²), predictive relevance (Q²), and the significance and stability of indicator weights.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a concrete, empirical demonstration of when to invert the default reflective rule, offering methodological guidance for researchers modeling complex constructs. It will advance the theoretical understanding of brand authenticity by framing it as a composite index rather than a latent trait, with direct implications for measurement and managerial diagnosis in branding and marketing.
proposals.novelty: While the formative-reflective distinction is known, systematically advocating for formative measurement for well-established psychological constructs—and providing an empirical testbed using brand authenticity—directly challenges a deep-seated disciplinary norm. It applies Henseler's advanced composite specification methods to a core theoretical problem in consumer psychology.

proposals.title: Inverse PLS-PM: A Computational Framework for Optimal Construct Design from a Target Structural Model
proposals.motivation: In structural equation modeling, researchers typically design measurement models (indicators) first and then estimate the structural model. This forward approach can lead to suboptimal construct operationalization, where the resulting structural relationships are weaker or different than theoretically intended. An inverse methodology is needed to ensure that the measurement model is optimally specified to recover a predefined, theoretically sound network of relationships, enhancing the validity and power of composite-based models.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given target structural model (path coefficients and latent variable correlations), there exists an optimal set of composite specifications (weights, number, and nature of indicators) that can be algorithmically derived, and this optimal measurement model will yield more accurate and statistically powerful parameter recovery in PLS-SEM than ad-hoc or purely theory-driven specifications.
proposals.method: The research will formalize the inverse PLS problem as a constrained optimization task. We will extend the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites within a computational search framework. Starting from a target variance-covariance matrix of constructs, we will employ iterative algorithms (e.g., genetic algorithms or simulated annealing) to search the space of possible measurement model specifications (indicator weights, loadings, and inter-correlations). The objective function will minimize the discrepancy between the target structural model and the model estimated via PLS-PM from data generated by candidate measurement models. The framework will be validated using Monte Carlo simulations across varied conditions (sample size, model complexity, composite types).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, computational method for construct design, shifting from a purely confirmatory to a design-oriented paradigm in composite-based SEM. It will offer researchers a systematic tool to strengthen the alignment between theory and measurement, potentially increasing the explanatory power of their models. The proposed algorithms and software implementation will represent a significant methodological advancement for marketing, information systems, and other fields reliant on PLS-PM.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature focuses on estimating structural models from given data, this proposal inverts the problem to design the measurement model for a desired structural outcome. It combines Henseler's refined composite specification with computational optimization techniques, a synthesis not previously explored in the SEM literature.

proposals.title: Robust Estimation for Formative Constructs: A Partial Least Squares Path Modeling Approach with Outlier-Resistant Weights
proposals.motivation: Formative measurement models are crucial in fields like marketing and information systems for modeling composite constructs, yet they are highly vulnerable to outliers in indicator data. Pre-2026 research on robust PLS has predominantly focused on reflective models, leaving a significant methodological gap. This gap hinders the reliable application of formative models in real-world data, which often contains influential extreme values.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating robust covariance estimation and iterative reweighting schemes into the PLS algorithm for formative constructs, the estimated indicator weights and subsequent path coefficients will be significantly less biased by outliers, leading to more valid model inferences.
proposals.method: The method builds upon the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. We will adapt the PLS algorithm by replacing the standard OLS regressions in the inner weight estimation and the outer weight estimation for formative blocks with robust alternatives, such as M-estimation or S-estimation. Specifically, we will employ an iterative reweighted least squares (IRLS) procedure within each PLS iteration, downweighting observations based on their residuals and leverage. The algorithm's performance will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations manipulating outlier type, percentage, and model complexity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a practical, robust PLS algorithm specifically designed for formative measurement models, directly enhancing the rigor of empirical studies using such constructs. It will extend the toolkit for structural equation modeling with composites, offering researchers a defensible method for handling non-normal, outlier-prone data without resorting to ad-hoc indicator removal.
proposals.novelty: While robust PLS exists, its application has been largely confined to reflective models. This proposal novelly targets the distinct vulnerability of formative measurement, where outliers can distort the very construction of the latent variable, and integrates robustness directly into the composite specification framework.

proposals.title: Diagnose and Adapt: A Multi-Method Robustness Framework for Higher-Order Constructs in PLS-SEM
proposals.motivation: Applied researchers in fields like marketing and information systems increasingly use PLS-SEM with complex higher-order constructs (HOCs) to model phenomena like brand authenticity. Real-world data often contains contamination (e.g., outliers, non-normal responses) which can severely bias PLS estimates, especially for formative HOCs. Current robust PLS methods are monolithic, applying a single technique regardless of the contamination's specific nature and location, potentially offering inadequate or even harmful protection.
proposals.hypothesis: A diagnostic and adaptive framework that first identifies the type and locus of data contamination and then selects a tailored robust estimation procedure will yield more accurate and stable parameter estimates for HOCs in PLS-SEM than applying any single robust method uniformly.
proposals.method: The framework integrates a diagnostic module using robust distance measures (e.g., Mahalanobis distance with Minimum Covariance Determinant) and residual analysis to classify contamination (vertical outliers, good/bad leverage points). A decision engine, informed by a comprehensive simulation study, will map diagnosed patterns to an ensemble of validated robust techniques (e.g., robust PLS path modeling with sign changes, iterative reweighting, or consistent PLSc). The simulation engine, built on the ADANCO platform, will validate the selection logic under controlled contamination in reflective-formative and repeated indicator HOC models. A cross-validation protocol will be provided for applied use.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a principled, generalizable toolkit for applied researchers to ensure their PLS-SEM results are not unduly influenced by anomalous data. It advances methodological rigor by moving from a one-size-fits-all to a fit-for-purpose robustness strategy, directly supporting Henseler's work on composite specification and model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While robust PLS techniques exist, the novel integration of a diagnostic phase with an adaptive selection mechanism, specifically tailored to the vulnerabilities of higher-order constructs, represents a significant departure from current practice.

proposals.title: Robustifying PLS Path Modeling: Integrating Minimum Covariance Determinant Estimators for Outlier-Resistant Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: Partial Least Squares (PLS) path modeling is widely used in marketing and composite-based SEM for prediction and exploratory analysis, but its covariance-based algorithms are sensitive to outliers. In applied fields like brand research, data often contains influential outliers that can distort parameter estimates and model conclusions. Pre-2026 robust covariance estimation techniques, like the Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD), are established in covariance-based SEM but not systematically integrated into the iterative PLS algorithm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing standard covariance calculations in the PLS algorithm's outer and inner approximations with robust MCD-based estimates will yield path coefficients and loadings that are less biased by outliers, without sacrificing the method's advantages for small samples and complex models.
proposals.method: The core technical approach is to modify the PLS-SEM algorithm (specifically, the mode B estimation for composites) by integrating the Fast-MCD estimator. In each iteration, where the algorithm calculates covariances between latent variable scores and indicators (outer model) and between latent variables (inner model), these moments will be estimated using the MCD. We will develop and test this 'MCD-PLS' specification within Henseler's consistent PLSc framework, comparing its performance against standard PLS and covariance-based robust SEM using Monte Carlo simulations with contaminated data. The implementation will leverage established R libraries (e.g., robustbase for MCD).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, robust variant of PLS-SEM, expanding the methodological toolkit for researchers analyzing non-normal data or data prone to outliers, particularly in marketing and management. It contributes to the 'design' of robust composites by offering a concrete, algorithm-level refinement, enhancing the reliability of conclusions in empirical studies using PLS.
proposals.novelty: While robust estimators exist for factor-based SEM, their integration into the iterative, variance-based PLS algorithm to protect both the measurement and structural models from outliers is a novel specification. This bridges the robust statistics literature with the specific composite model architecture advanced in Henseler's recent work.

proposals.title: Robust Univariate PLS Regression: A Simplified Specification and Pedagogical Tool for Applied Researchers
proposals.motivation: Robust Partial Least Squares (PLS) methods, such as robust PLS path modeling, are crucial for handling non-normal data and outliers in fields like marketing and brand research. However, their multivariate complexity and intricate specification can be a barrier for applied researchers and students. A simplified, univariate robust PLS regression would provide an accessible entry point for understanding robust PLS principles and a practical tool for common single-outcome prediction problems.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust univariate PLS regression, derived as a special case of the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites, will maintain the robustness properties of its multivariate counterpart while offering superior interpretability and computational simplicity for single-outcome scenarios.
proposals.method: The research will derive the model specification by constraining the robust PLS path model to a single endogenous composite. We will implement the algorithm by integrating a robust covariance estimation method (e.g., the Fast Consistent Minimum Covariance Determinant (FAST-MCD) estimator) into the PLS-SEM algorithm's inner and outer approximation steps, specifically for the single-outcome case. The method's performance will be empirically validated against OLS and standard PLS regression using Monte Carlo simulations with contaminated data and applied to a pre-2026 brand authenticity dataset to demonstrate practical utility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clearly specified, easy-to-implement robust regression tool for applied researchers dealing with outlier-prone, single-outcome data. It will also serve as a vital pedagogical bridge, helping students and new researchers grasp the core concepts of robust PLS before tackling full path models.
proposals.novelty: While robust PLS for path models exists, a dedicated, formally specified, and algorithmically streamlined version for univariate regression is novel. It explicitly connects the robust PLS-SEM framework to the classic regression problem, filling a gap in the toolkit for robust predictive analysis.

proposals.title: Modeling Emergent Constructs: A Specification and Test for Directionality in Composite-Based Structural Equation Models
proposals.motivation: In composite-based SEM (e.g., PLS-PM), the directionality between a composite and its indicators is a critical, yet often arbitrary, model specification choice. Researchers must decide a priori if the composite is formed by (effect) or reflective of (cause) its indicators, a decision with major implications for model validity and interpretation. Current practice relies heavily on theoretical reasoning, lacking robust empirical methods to test or estimate this directionality, potentially leading to model misspecification.
proposals.hypothesis: The directionality between a composite and its indicators is not a fixed property but can be empirically informed by analyzing the pattern of covariances between the composite's indicators and external criteria within the nomological network. Specifically, the emergent nature (composite as effect) vs. latent variable nature (composite as cause) of a construct manifests in distinct, testable covariance structures.
proposals.method: We will extend the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites to incorporate a flexible, estimable parameter (δ) that captures the directionality. This parameter will be embedded within a modified PLS-PM algorithm, where the inner weighting scheme is adapted to estimate δ by minimizing the discrepancy between the implied and empirical covariances of the indicators with other model constructs. The estimation will be validated using a Monte Carlo simulation study to assess power and Type I error rates under known population conditions. Finally, we will apply the method to a pre-2026 marketing dataset (e.g., brand authenticity) to demonstrate its practical utility.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal, testable framework for the emergent/reflective distinction in composite modeling, moving the field beyond purely conceptual guidelines. It will deliver a concrete, implementable extension to PLS-PM software, allowing researchers to empirically scrutinize a foundational model assumption. This enhances the rigor of composite-based analysis in fields like marketing and information systems.
proposals.novelty: While the formative/reflective distinction is widely discussed, no pre-2026 method within the composite modeling paradigm allows for the empirical estimation and statistical testing of directionality as a model parameter. Our approach integrates this test directly into the Henseler-Ogasawara specification and PLS-PM estimation routine, making it a native part of the model-building process.

proposals.title: Causal Discovery for Model Specification: A Constraint-Based Algorithmic Approach to Inform PLS Path Models
proposals.motivation: Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) is widely used in marketing and composite-based research, but its validity critically depends on correct model specification. Current practice relies heavily on theoretical justification and researcher intuition, which can introduce bias and misspecification. This project addresses the need for a more data-driven, objective method to suggest initial path model structures, enhancing the robustness of PLS analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constraint-based causal discovery algorithms, applied to observational data, can generate plausible and theoretically coherent initial model specifications for PLS-SEM that outperform or usefully complement researcher-specified models derived solely from literature.
proposals.method: We will employ the PC and FCI algorithms, established constraint-based causal search methods, to analyze observational datasets from marketing and brand research. These algorithms use conditional independence tests to suggest causal structures (directed acyclic graphs). The output will be translated into a formative and/or reflective measurement model and structural path model compatible with Henseler-Ogasawara composite specification. The process will be formalized into a reproducible workflow, and the resulting models will be compared against benchmark theoretical models using standard PLS-SEM fit and predictive metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, algorithm-assisted workflow for PLS model specification, reducing subjective bias in the initial design phase. It will provide methodological guidance on integrating causal discovery, a technique from machine learning and statistics, into the composite-based SEM paradigm, potentially improving model validity and discovery.
proposals.novelty: While causal discovery is known in statistics, its systematic application to inform the specification of composites and paths in PLS-SEM—a variance-based, prediction-oriented method—is novel. This bridges a gap between constraint-based causal search and soft-modeling SEM traditions.

proposals.title: Parsing Limbic Integrity: A Bifactor Structural Equation Model for Disentangling General and Specific Neural Contributions to Cognitive-Affective Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: In neuropsychology and consumer neuroscience, constructs like emotional regulation or brand attachment are theorized to arise from both the integrated function of the limbic system (general integrity) and the specific contributions of structures like the hippocampus (HC) and working memory (WM) networks. Pre-2026, composite models in SEM often confound these sources of variance, limiting the precise attribution of neural mechanisms to behavioral outcomes. This ambiguity hinders the development of targeted interventions in clinical psychology or nuanced branding strategies in marketing.
proposals.hypothesis: A psychometric bifactor model, specifying a general 'limbic integrity' factor alongside orthogonal specific factors for WM and HC function, will provide superior model fit and more precise parameter estimates than traditional correlated-factors or higher-order models, revealing unique predictive pathways from each factor to external criteria like affective dysregulation or brand authenticity perceptions.
proposals.method: We will employ a refined Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composite models within a covariance-based SEM framework (e.g., using LISREL or the `lavaan` package in R) to implement a bifactor model. The general and specific factors will be modeled as composites of relevant neural indicators (e.g., fMRI activation, volumetric data, or connectivity scores). Model identification will be ensured using the SL approach for scaling. We will compare the bifactor specification against alternative models using fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) and test the unique effects of each factor on validated outcome measures via path coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological blueprint for decomposing neural contributions in structural brain-behavior models, advancing measurement theory in neuroimaging. It will yield substantive insights into whether phenomena like 'brand authenticity' or 'emotional resilience' are driven more by general limbic integration or specific subsystem functions, informing both basic neuroscience and applied fields like neuromarketing.
proposals.novelty: While bifactor models are established in psychometrics, their application to model neural composites within Henseler's SEM framework for composites is novel. This moves beyond modeling latent variables as common factors to explicitly model theoretically defined neural composites, allowing a clean separation of general and specific effects not possible with standard techniques.

proposals.title: Inverse Prediction in PLS-SEM: A Framework for Deriving Required Input Patterns to Achieve Target Latent Variable Scores
proposals.motivation: Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) is extensively used in fields like marketing and information systems for predictive analysis and theory testing. However, its application is predominantly forward-looking, predicting outcomes from inputs. A significant managerial and research gap exists in solving the inverse problem: determining the specific configurations of observed indicator values (e.g., marketing mix elements, product features) necessary to achieve a desired target score on a key latent construct (e.g., brand authenticity, customer satisfaction). This capability would transform PLS-SEM from a diagnostic tool into a prescriptive one for strategic design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, for a given estimated PLS path model, it is possible to formulate and computationally solve an inverse prediction problem to identify—within the bounds of model constraints and measurement theory—the set of observable indicator patterns that yield a pre-specified profile of latent variable scores.
proposals.method: The research will formalize the inverse PLS problem as a constrained optimization task. Building upon the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites, we will derive the necessary equations linking target latent scores to required outer weights and indicator values. The core algorithm will involve solving a system of equations defined by the inner and outer model estimations, potentially employing numerical methods like gradient-based optimization or quadratic programming to find feasible indicator solutions that minimize the discrepancy from the target latent scores while respecting the model's structural relationships and measurement assumptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological extension to PLS-SEM, enabling 'what-if' and goal-seeking analysis directly within the SEM framework. It will offer researchers and practitioners in design, marketing, and composites research a systematic tool for deriving actionable input requirements to meet strategic targets, thereby bridging the gap between model prediction and intervention design.
proposals.novelty: While inverse problems are studied in engineering and machine learning, their formal integration into the PLS-SEM framework, particularly for composite models, is unexplored. This approach moves beyond standard PLS prediction and importance-performance analysis to offer a direct solution for prescriptive indicator-level design.

proposals.title: A Composite-Based Approach to Longitudinal and Panel Data: Extending PLS Path Modeling with Time and Cross-Sectional Dependencies
proposals.motivation: The analysis of longitudinal and panel data is crucial in fields like marketing and organizational research to understand dynamic processes. However, traditional PLS-SEM, while powerful for composite-based models, lacks native capabilities to model temporal dependencies (e.g., autoregressive effects) and cross-sectional dependencies (e.g., peer effects) inherent in such data. This forces researchers to use workarounds or switch to covariance-based SEM, which may not align with their composite model specification needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally integrating time-lagged relationships and cross-sectional correlation structures into the PLS algorithm and model specification, we can create a valid and consistent composite-based method for panel data that outperforms ad-hoc approaches in recovering true longitudinal parameters.
proposals.method: We will extend the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites to formally include lagged composites (e.g., Y_t = f(Y_{t-1}, X_t)). The PLS algorithm will be modified to estimate these time-structured relationships iteratively. For cross-sectional dependencies, we will incorporate spatial or network weight matrices into the inner model estimation, drawing on pre-2026 spatial econometrics. The method's properties will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations comparing bias, consistency, and power against existing PLS practices and covariance-based alternatives.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, native PLS framework for longitudinal and panel data analysis, enabling researchers to test dynamic theories using composite models. It will yield concrete guidelines and software implementations (e.g., for `semPLS` or `cSEM`) for modeling temporal and cross-sectional effects, directly benefiting fields like brand tracking and innovation diffusion studies.
proposals.novelty: While time-series PLS exists, a comprehensive framework for unbalanced panel data with both temporal *and* cross-sectional dependencies within the composite model paradigm is novel. The integration of spatial/network dependencies into the PLS algorithm itself represents a significant advancement beyond simple control variables.

proposals.title: A Rigorous Examination of Moderators in Technology Adoption: Applying PLS-Based Moderation Procedures to the UTAUT Framework
proposals.motivation: The Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) is a dominant framework for explaining technology adoption, yet its core propositions rely heavily on moderation effects (e.g., age, gender, experience). Prior empirical tests of these moderators have often relied on traditional regression-based subgroup analysis, which can be inefficient and lacks the flexibility for complex model structures. With the widespread use of Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) for analyzing UTAUT, a standardized, rigorous approach for testing its moderators within the PLS context is needed to improve methodological clarity and result reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced PLS-based moderation procedures (specifically, the orthogonalizing and two-stage approaches) to the UTAUT model will provide a more statistically robust and nuanced understanding of moderator effects (e.g., age, gender, voluntariness) on the relationships between performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, and behavioral intention.
proposals.method: We will employ a composite model specification for the UTAUT constructs, consistent with Henseler's recent refinements. Using survey data on the adoption of a specific technology (e.g., a smart service), we will implement and compare multiple PLS moderation techniques. This includes the orthogonalizing approach for continuous moderators and the product indicator approach with latent variable scores for categorical moderators, all executed within the ADANCO or SmartPLS software. The analysis will follow a systematic procedure: 1) validating the measurement model, 2) estimating the main UTAUT path model, and 3) testing the significance and effect size of each hypothesized interaction term using bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, step-by-step blueprint for researchers to correctly test UTAUT's moderation hypotheses using PLS-SEM, enhancing methodological rigor in the information systems and marketing fields. It will yield empirical insights into whether the strength of UTAUT relationships is contingent on key moderators in a contemporary context, potentially refining the theory's application.
proposals.novelty: While PLS is commonly used with UTAUT, a comprehensive and comparative application of modern PLS interaction detection methods (beyond simple multi-group analysis) to the full set of UTAUT moderators represents a novel methodological contribution, bridging Henseler's expertise in composite modeling with a seminal theoretical framework.

proposals.title: A Latent Moderated Structural Equations (LMS) Approach for PLS-Path Modeling: Specification, Estimation, and Application in Brand Research
proposals.motivation: Testing interaction effects between latent constructs is crucial in fields like marketing and consumer psychology, where moderating relationships (e.g., brand authenticity moderating the effect of AI-disclosure on attitude) are common. While covariance-based SEM offers the Latent Moderated Structural Equations (LMS) method for this purpose, its application within Partial Least Squares (PLS) path modeling remains underdeveloped. This gap limits researchers who prefer PLS for complex, composite-based models in exploratory research or with non-normal data.
proposals.hypothesis: The refined Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites can be successfully integrated with a product-indicator approach to enable the consistent and efficient estimation of latent interaction effects within the PLS-SEM framework.
proposals.method: We will extend the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites to incorporate interaction terms. The method involves creating product indicators for the latent moderator and its interacting construct, following pre-2026 best practices for two-stage approaches. We will implement this within a PLS algorithm, adapting the estimation to handle the non-linear relationships. The approach will be validated via Monte Carlo simulation, comparing its performance (bias, power, Type I error) to covariance-based LMS and existing PLS interaction techniques, before applying it to a substantive dataset on AI-disclosed brand voice.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, methodologically sound procedure for testing latent interactions in PLS-SEM, directly enhancing the analytical toolkit for composite-based models. It will offer applied researchers in marketing and beyond a clear blueprint for model specification and estimation, bridging a significant gap between covariance-based and variance-based SEM techniques.
proposals.novelty: While product-indicator approaches exist for PLS, a formal integration with the Henseler-Ogasawara specification to create a dedicated LMS-PLS procedure is novel. This moves beyond simple two-stage least squares approximations, aiming for a more integrated estimation within the PLS algorithm itself.

proposals.title: A Robust Bootstrap Test for Moderation Effects in Composite-based Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: In PLS-SEM, assessing the significance of moderation effects is crucial for fields like marketing and brand research, where interaction terms are common. Traditional approaches, such as the product indicator approach, often rely on distributional assumptions that may not hold, leading to unreliable p-values and confidence intervals. A robust, assumption-free method for significance testing is needed to improve the rigor of model evaluation in applied research.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying non-parametric bootstrapping procedures specifically tailored for the Henseler-Ogasawara specification of composite models will yield more accurate and robust significance tests for moderation effects compared to standard asymptotic methods.
proposals.method: The research will extend the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites to explicitly include interaction terms. We will develop and implement a dedicated bootstrapping algorithm that resamples cases to generate an empirical distribution for the moderation effect path coefficient. This involves calculating the interaction term for each bootstrap sample, estimating the model, and constructing bias-corrected and accelerated (BCa) confidence intervals. The method's performance will be validated via Monte Carlo simulations against standard t-tests across varying sample sizes, distributional shapes, and model misspecifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, accessible bootstrapping protocol for testing moderation in composite models, directly enhancing the methodological toolkit for PLS-SEM researchers. It will increase the reliability of conclusions drawn from interaction effects in applied studies, particularly in Henseler's domains of marketing and brand management.
proposals.novelty: While bootstrapping is common in PLS-SEM for main effects, a systematic and refined bootstrapping approach explicitly designed for moderation effects within the specific framework of the Henseler-Ogasawara composite specification represents a novel methodological advancement.

proposals.title: Reversing the Causal Arrow: A Composite Model Testing Behavioral Intention as a Moderator of Its Antecedents' Effect on Technology Use
proposals.motivation: The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) and related theories posit a well-established causal chain where beliefs and attitudes lead to behavioral intention, which in turn drives actual use. However, pre-2026 literature largely treats this as a linear, unidirectional process, potentially overlooking a more dynamic reality. This study challenges this static view by investigating if the formation of a strong behavioral intention fundamentally alters how its antecedents subsequently influence behavior, a critical question for predicting and designing effective technology interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The central hypothesis is that the strength of an individual's behavioral intention (e.g., to use a new software) moderates the relationship between its established antecedents (e.g., perceived usefulness, ease of use) and subsequent actual use behavior. Specifically, we posit that for individuals with strong intentions, the direct effect of these antecedents on use will be attenuated.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal field study with two waves of data collection (intention measurement followed by behavioral tracking). The core analysis will use a composite-based structural equation modeling (SEM) approach, specifically the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for creating and testing the latent interaction term. The moderator variable (behavioral intention) will be specified as a composite, interacting with a composite formed from its antecedents (e.g., a composite of perceived usefulness and ease of use) to predict the actual use outcome. Analysis will be conducted using consistent PLS (PLSc) algorithms in ADANCO or SmartPLS to obtain unbiased parameter estimates for the interaction effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, non-recursive perspective to technology adoption theories by empirically testing intention as a moderator, not just a mediator. It will provide methodological guidance on specifying and testing such 'inverse' moderating relationships within composite-based SEM. For practitioners, it offers insights into when intervention on beliefs is most critical versus when managing intention itself becomes paramount.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the conventional causal role of intention within dominant behavioral theories and formally testing this inversion using a refined composite model specification. This moves beyond merely establishing intention as a predictor to understanding it as a system state that qualitatively changes the drivers of behavior.

proposals.title: Consistent Partial Least Squares for Hierarchical Component Models and Nonlinear Effects: A Generalized Algorithmic Framework
proposals.motivation: The consistent PLS (PLSc) algorithm has become a standard for correcting bias in composite-based structural equation modeling, but its application remains largely confined to traditional, linear path models. In fields like marketing and organizational research, complex model specifications—such as hierarchical component models (HCM) for multilevel data and models incorporating nonlinear (e.g., quadratic, interaction) effects—are increasingly necessary for valid theory testing. The lack of a consistent estimation framework for these advanced model types limits the methodological rigor and statistical validity of empirical research using PLS.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of the consistent PLS correction can be formally extended and algorithmically implemented for models with hierarchical components and for models containing specific nonlinear relationships, yielding consistent parameter estimates for these more complex structures.
proposals.method: Building on the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites, we will first develop a generalized correction formula for the composite correlation matrix that accounts for the nested structure of hierarchical components. Second, for nonlinear effects, we will integrate the consistent PLS estimator with the two-stage approach that uses latent variable scores, ensuring consistency in both stages. The algorithmic development will be implemented in R and tested via extensive Monte Carlo simulations comparing the proposed method's bias, efficiency, and coverage to traditional PLS and covariance-based SEM benchmarks under various conditions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified, consistent estimation framework for a broader class of composite-based SEMs, directly enhancing the analytical toolkit for researchers in marketing, management, and information systems. It will offer concrete guidelines and open-source software for specifying and estimating complex models with hierarchical or nonlinear effects without sacrificing consistency, thereby improving the reliability of conclusions drawn from such models.
proposals.novelty: While PLSc is well-established for linear models, its systematic extension to formally correct hierarchical component models and to integrate seamlessly with established nonlinear modeling techniques within the PLS paradigm represents a novel and non-trivial advancement.

proposals.title: A Monte Carlo Evaluation of Confirmatory Composite Analysis Fit Indices: Robustness Across Model Specifications and Data Conditions
proposals.motivation: The widespread adoption of Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) for composite-based models necessitates reliable global goodness-of-fit (GoF) tests to assess model validity. While confirmatory composite analysis (CCA) and tests like the Standardized Root Mean Squared Residual (SRMR) have been proposed, their empirical performance under diverse, realistic conditions—such as non-normal data, small sample sizes, and complex model structures—remains insufficiently understood. This gap limits researchers' confidence in using these tests for rigorous model evaluation in fields like marketing and information systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance (Type I error rate and statistical power) of novel confirmatory fit tests for composite models, such as the SRMR bootstrap-based test, is significantly influenced by data conditions (e.g., skewness, sample size) and model specification characteristics (e.g., number of indicators, composite correlations).
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study, systematically manipulating factors including sample size (from 50 to 1000), indicator distribution (normal, skewed), number of indicators per composite, composite correlation strength, and model complexity (number of composites). The core technical approach will be to generate data according to the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composite models, estimate models using PLS-SEM (via the `semPLS` or `cSEM` packages in R), and compute the SRMR and other emerging fit indices. Their performance will be evaluated by comparing empirical rejection rates against nominal alpha levels for Type I error and calculating power under misspecified models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide clear, evidence-based guidelines for applied researchers on the conditions under which specific fit tests are reliable. It will contribute to the methodological literature on PLS-SEM by offering a comprehensive validation framework for confirmatory fit assessment, thereby strengthening the rigor of composite model evaluation in practice.
proposals.novelty: While simulation studies on PLS exist, this proposal focuses specifically on the nascent area of confirmatory fit testing for composites, integrating the refined Henseler-Ogasawara specification to generate data that accurately reflects the composite model's properties, which is a non-trivial advancement over prior simulations often based on factor models.

proposals.title: Robustness in the Niche: A Finite-Sample Corrected PLS-SEM Algorithm for Very Small Sample Sizes
proposals.motivation: Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) is widely promoted for small sample sizes, yet its performance and validity in extremely small-N contexts (e.g., N < 30, or even N < 20) remain critically under-examined. Such contexts are common in specialized engineering research, early-stage entrepreneurial studies, and niche B2B marketing where populations are intrinsically small. Current PLS-SEM guidelines offer little robust methodological guidance, risking unreliable parameter estimates and invalid model tests in these important research domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a PLS-SEM variant incorporating finite-sample corrections to the path weighting scheme and bootstrap procedures will yield significantly more accurate parameter estimates and model validations for very small sample sizes (N < 30) compared to standard PLS-SEM algorithms.
proposals.method: The research will develop and validate a novel PLS-SEM algorithm. First, we will refine the Henseler-Ogasawara specification to integrate a finite-sample bias correction directly into the iterative estimation of latent variable scores. Second, we will adapt the consistent PLS (PLSc) algorithm for very small N, focusing on robust correlation estimation for correction. Third, we will develop and test a tailored bootstrap procedure (e.g., a stabilized bootstrap with moment correction) for significance testing. Validation will employ extensive Monte Carlo simulations across varied model structures (based on composite specifications), followed by empirical application in a small-N brand authenticity study, mirroring the researcher's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematically validated PLS-SEM protocol for very small-N research, complete with concrete guidelines and software implementation recommendations. It will empower researchers in fields like composites engineering and specialized marketing to apply PLS-SEM with greater confidence and rigor when populations are inherently limited.
proposals.novelty: While small-N capability is a known PLS-SEM feature, the explicit development and validation of a *finite-sample corrected* algorithm targeting N < 30 is novel. This moves beyond mere rule-of-thumb recommendations to a principled, statistical correction of the core estimation mechanics.

proposals.title: A Simulation-Based Benchmark of Bootstrap and Permutation Methods for Establishing Measurement and Structural Invariance in Composite-Based SEM
proposals.motivation: In structural equation modeling (SEM), particularly in marketing and composite-based research, establishing measurement invariance or model homogeneity across groups (e.g., cultures, segments) is crucial for valid comparison. Traditional multi-group analysis uses likelihood ratio tests to reject a null of difference, but confirming equivalence—inverting the null to affirm similarity—requires different statistical tools. Pre-2026, methods like bootstrap confidence intervals for parameter differences and permutation tests have been proposed for this purpose, but their relative performance, robustness, and power under realistic conditions (e.g., non-normal data, small samples, model misspecification) lack a systematic comparison.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that permutation-based tests for equivalence will demonstrate superior robustness to violations of distributional assumptions, while bootstrap confidence interval approaches will offer greater statistical power for detecting strict equivalence under conditions of multivariate normality and correctly specified composite models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating factors such as sample size, distribution (normal, skewed, heavy-tailed), number of indicators, and magnitude of permissible non-invariance. The core algorithms benchmarked are (1) a percentile bootstrap approach for the confidence interval of the largest parameter difference across groups (aligned with the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites) and (2) a permutation test that constructs a null distribution of model fit indices (e.g., SRMR) under the hypothesis of group homogeneity. Performance will be evaluated via Type I error rates (when models are truly equivalent) and statistical power (when small, pre-defined differences exist).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide applied researchers in marketing and beyond with clear, evidence-based guidelines for choosing the most appropriate method to statistically affirm model equivalence. It will extend the methodological toolkit for composite-based SEM by rigorously validating non-parametric approaches for invariance testing, thereby strengthening the validity of cross-group comparisons in brand, consumer, and organizational research.
proposals.novelty: While both bootstrap and permutation methods exist, a direct benchmarking study focused specifically on their efficacy for *confirming* model equivalence (rather than detecting differences) in the context of composite models is novel. This synthesis directly addresses the practical need for robust equivalence testing, a critical step often glossed over in multi-group SEM applications.

proposals.title: Equating for Invariance: A Framework for Multi-Group Analysis with Differently Scaled Indicators
proposals.motivation: In cross-cultural or longitudinal research using structural equation modeling (SEM), the strict assumption of identically scaled indicators for Measurement Invariance/Growth Analysis (MGA) is often violated. This occurs when composite indicators are built from different source items, use different response scales, or undergo group-specific standardization, preventing valid group comparisons. Current solutions, like partial invariance testing, are insufficient when the scaling mismatch is systematic, limiting the application of SEM in real-world marketing, organizational, and composite materials research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting psychometric test equating functions—specifically linear and equipercentile equating—to link the scales of latent variables via a set of anchor indicators, we can establish a common metric for MGA, thereby relaxing the requirement for identically scaled manifest variables across groups.
proposals.method: The method extends the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites within a multi-group SEM framework. First, a subset of indicators is identified as 'anchors' based on content validity and preliminary stability tests. Using these anchors, we estimate group-specific linking functions (e.g., mean-sigma linear equating) to transform the parameters (loadings, intercepts) of the non-anchor indicators onto a common scale. The transformed model is then subjected to a refined sequence of invariance tests (configural, metric, scalar) using bootstrap or permutation methods to account for the equating error. Simulation studies will validate the approach under varying degrees of scaling discrepancy and anchor quality.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a practical, statistically-grounded framework for conducting meaningful multi-group comparisons with real-world, messily scaled data. It directly contributes to methodological literature in SEM and psychometrics and offers applied researchers in fields like international marketing and cross-cultural composites research a tool to test hypotheses previously deemed infeasible.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal cross-disciplinary analogy, applying the well-established solution of test equating from educational measurement to solve a persistent scaling problem in covariance-based SEM and composite-based SEM (PLSc), which has not been systematically explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Fusing Mixture Modeling and Multi-Group Analysis: An Algorithm for Simultaneous Latent Class Discovery and Scale Calibration in Structural Equation Models
proposals.motivation: In pooled datasets from diverse sources (e.g., cross-cultural studies, meta-analyses, or multi-source brand tracking), latent subpopulations often exist, and indicators may have different scales across these unknown groups due to varied measurement or standardization practices. Pre-2026 multi-group analysis (MGA) and finite mixture SEM require a priori group knowledge or assume measurement invariance, failing in this exploratory context. This leads to biased parameter estimates and missed substantive insights.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative algorithm that simultaneously clusters cases based on model parameters and residual structures, while estimating group-specific scaling factors, can correctly recover latent subpopulations and yield unbiased structural parameter estimates, even when measurement scales differ heterogeneously across undiscovered groups.
proposals.method: We will develop an Expectation-Maximization (EM)-type algorithm. In the E-step, cases are probabilistically assigned to groups using a machine learning clustering approach (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Model) on a feature vector comprising their case-wise contributions to model parameters and standardized residuals from a pooled model. In the M-step, for each latent group, we estimate a multi-group SEM using the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites, incorporating group-specific scaling factors (variance corrections) for indicators. The algorithm iterates until cluster stability and model convergence are achieved, refining group assignments and parameters simultaneously.
proposals.contribution: This provides a rigorous methodological framework for exploratory MGA in pooled, messy data common in marketing and composite-based SEM. It extends the Henseler-Ogasawara specification to unknown-group contexts, offering researchers a tool to discover segments and calibrate scales without pre-specified grouping variables. The method enhances the robustness of findings in international business, consumer psychology, and interdisciplinary research using composite models.
proposals.novelty: The integration of clustering on model-driven features with within-cluster scale calibration in a single estimation procedure is novel. Unlike existing finite mixture SEM, it explicitly relaxes the assumption of invariant indicator scales across latent classes, addressing a pervasive but unmodeled issue in pooled data analysis.

proposals.title: Network Stability Permutation Tests for Multi-Group Analysis of Formative Measurement Models
proposals.motivation: Validating formative measurement models across groups (MGA) remains a critical, unresolved challenge in structural equation modeling (SEM). Pre-2026, methods like MICOM for composite models assess invariance of composite scores and weights, but lack a direct test for the stability of the entire formative network's configuration. This gap impedes rigorous cross-group comparisons in fields like marketing and information systems, where constructs (e.g., brand perception) are often formatively defined.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability of a formative measurement model's network configuration—its specific pattern of indicator weights and structural relationships—can be rigorously assessed for invariance across groups using permutation tests adapted from ecological network stability analysis. This approach will provide a more appropriate validation framework than methods reliant on indicator covariance.
proposals.method: We adapt the Jacobian matrix analysis of ecological networks, which evaluates system stability based on interaction strengths. For a formative model, we construct a weighted adjacency matrix representing the formative measurement and structural paths. Using a permutation-based approach (e.g., a double bootstrap or randomization test), we will generate a null distribution of network stability metrics (e.g., the leading eigenvalue of the system's Jacobian matrix) under the hypothesis of no group differences. The observed difference in network configurations between groups will be tested against this distribution, providing a p-value for the invariance of the formative network structure.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, theoretically-grounded statistical test for the multi-group validation of formative measurement models, directly addressing a key limitation in composite-based SEM. It will offer applied researchers a robust tool for ensuring measurement equivalence in cross-cultural or comparative studies using formative constructs, enhancing the rigor of their analyses.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by directly importing and adapting the concept of network stability from theoretical ecology to psychometrics, moving beyond covariance-based invariance testing to evaluate the equivalence of the formative system's *configuration*, which is its defining characteristic.

proposals.title: Extending the Henseler–Ogasawara Specification and Multi-Group Analysis Tests to Formative Measurement Models: A Monte Carlo Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: The Henseler–Ogasawara (H-O) specification for composite models and associated multi-group analysis (MGA) tests represent a significant advancement for variance-based SEM (e.g., PLS-SEM), offering a statistically rigorous framework for comparing composite constructs across groups. However, their development and validation have primarily focused on models with reflective (effect) indicators. In practice, formative (causal) measurement models are crucial in fields like marketing (e.g., index construction) and information systems, yet rigorous statistical tests for comparing such formative constructs across groups remain underdeveloped. Extending these tests is essential to ensure the validity of group comparisons in a wide array of empirical research.
proposals.hypothesis: The core hypothesis is that the H-O specification can be successfully adapted for formative measurement models, and that the derived MGA tests (e.g., the asymptotic test based on the H-O specification) will provide valid Type I error control and adequate statistical power for detecting true parameter differences between groups when the constructs are formatively measured.
proposals.method: The research will employ a Monte Carlo simulation study, the standard pre-2026 method for validating SEM techniques. We will extend the H-O specification to explicitly model formative constructs, treating them as composite variables formed as weighted sums of their indicators. The simulation will manipulate key factors: number of groups (2), group sample sizes (small to large), number of formative indicators, correlation between indicators, and magnitude of group differences in the formative weights and structural path coefficients. Data will be generated accordingly, and the extended H-O MGA tests will be applied. Performance will be evaluated by comparing empirical Type I error rates to nominal levels (e.g., 0.05) and by analyzing statistical power.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, rigorous statistical framework for conducting multi-group analysis with formative measurement models within the PLS-SEM paradigm. It will directly extend the methodological toolkit for researchers in marketing, management information systems, and other fields that rely on formative constructs. The study will offer clear guidelines and software implementation recommendations (e.g., for `cSEM` or `semPLS`) for applied researchers.
proposals.novelty: While the H-O specification is established for reflective/composite models, its explicit adaptation and rigorous testing for purely formative measurement models is novel. This addresses a critical gap, as formative models require distinct handling of measurement error and construct representation, making the extension non-trivial and its performance an open empirical question.

proposals.title: Power and Precision in SEM: A Taxonomy and Simulation Study for Detecting Causal Model Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Structural equation modeling (SEM) is a cornerstone of causal analysis in fields like marketing and composites research, yet power analysis for model fit remains blunt. Current approaches, such as those based on RMSEA, treat misspecification as a monolithic concept, failing to inform researchers whether their study is adequately powered to detect specific, theoretically critical errors like a missing mediation path or an incorrectly specified composite. This ambiguity undermines the validity of conclusions drawn from accepted models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical power to reject a misspecified model varies systematically and predictably across a formal taxonomy of causal perturbations, and that this relationship can be quantified to provide precise, actionable power guidelines for empirical researchers.
proposals.method: Building on the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites, we will first formalize a taxonomy of perturbations: parameter magnitude shifts, edge existence, mediated path alterations, and global topology changes. We will then implement a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation framework in R (using lavaan and simsem). For each perturbation class and level, we will generate data from the 'true' model, fit the hypothesized (perturbed) model, and record the sensitivity of fit indices (chi-square, CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) and novel tests. Power curves will be established as a function of sample size, model complexity, and perturbation severity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first systematic power guide for specific model misspecifications, moving beyond generic effect size measures. We will deliver open-source software (R package) that allows researchers to conduct targeted power analysis for their hypothesized model and the specific causal discrepancies they deem most critical to avoid.
proposals.novelty: While power analysis for SEM exists, no pre-2026 work offers a perturbation-type-driven framework. The novelty lies in shifting the focus from 'is there misfit?' to 'can I detect *this specific kind* of misfit?', directly linking power to the substantive theory being tested.

proposals.title: Targeted Path Sensitivity Analysis (TPSA): A Structural Engineering Analogy for Testing Specific Parameter Differences in Composite Structural Equation Models
proposals.motivation: In structural equation modeling (SEM), particularly with composite models, researchers often test for group differences or model modifications using omnibus tests like the chi-square difference. These global tests can lack nuance, failing to identify if a significant effect is driven by a specific, theoretically critical path or is diffusely spread across many parameters. This limits the precision of theory testing, especially in fields like marketing and information systems where specific brand or technology adoption pathways are of central interest.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting sensitivity analysis techniques from structural engineering—where stress is applied to specific components to find critical failure points—will yield a testing framework that can more powerfully and informatively detect and localize specific parameter differences in composite SEM models than existing global fit comparisons.
proposals.method: The method extends the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. We will develop an algorithm that applies targeted constraints (e.g., equality constraints across groups) to a single, theoretically specified path coefficient. The framework then quantifies the resulting 'disproportionate impact' on model explanatory power, measured by a localized loss in variance accounted for (VAF) or a tailored discrepancy function, relative to the baseline model and the fully constrained model. This creates a sensitivity profile for each path. The procedure will be integrated into a permutation-based testing routine to assess the statistical significance of the localized sensitivity measure.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel diagnostic tool for researchers using PLS-SEM and covariance-based SEM with composites, enabling more nuanced theory testing by pinpointing which specific causal pathways are responsible for observed model differences. It contributes to methodological rigor in fields reliant on path models (e.g., marketing, behavioral research) by moving beyond 'if' a difference exists to 'where' it is most critical. The work will also enrich the literature on model specification and comparison.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis exists in statistics, the direct conceptual and technical translation of component-level stress-testing from structural engineering to the problem of path coefficient comparison in SEM is novel. The approach moves beyond global model fit indices to a targeted, path-specific inferential framework, which is not a standard feature in pre-2026 SEM software.

proposals.title: Power Analysis for Specific Parameter Differences in Composite-based Structural Equation Modeling: A Monte Carlo Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: In applied fields like marketing and information systems, researchers using Partial Least Squares (PLS) and composite-based Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) often test hypotheses about specific path coefficients. However, current power analysis methods and guidelines (e.g., using R-squared effect sizes) focus on the overall model's predictive power, not the sensitivity to detect a specific, theoretically meaningful difference between two path coefficients or a deviation from a null value. This gap can lead to underpowered studies for key hypotheses or misinterpretation of non-significant results.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical power to detect a specific, constrained parameter difference (e.g., path A > path B) in composite models is not adequately captured by global model effect size measures and is significantly influenced by the composite model's specification, indicator loadings, and correlation structure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study building on the refined Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. The experimental design will systematically vary model parameters (e.g., loading patterns, inter-construct correlations, path magnitudes) and sample sizes. For each condition, we will generate data, estimate models using PLS-SEM (via `semPLS` or `cSEM`), and perform constrained model tests (e.g., bootstrapped difference tests or Wald tests on linear constraints). The primary outcome is empirical power—the proportion of iterations correctly rejecting the false null hypothesis for the specified parameter difference. We will analyze results to develop practical power tables and heuristics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first dedicated framework for a priori and post-hoc power analysis for testing specific parameter differences in composite-based SEM. It will offer applied researchers clear guidelines and potentially software routines to design more robust studies. The work will refine methodological best practices in fields reliant on PLS-SEM, enhancing the validity of comparative hypothesis testing.
proposals.novelty: While power analysis for global model fit in covariance-based SEM exists, a focused investigation on the power of constrained parameter tests within the composite model paradigm, explicitly considering the implications of the Henseler-Ogasawara specification, is novel. It moves beyond assessing overall model explanatory power to the precision of specific theoretical comparisons.

proposals.title: Extending the Heterotrait-Monotrait Ratio of Correlations: A New Specification for Assessing Discriminant Validity in Composite Models
proposals.motivation: The Heterotrait-Monotrait (HTMT) ratio of correlations has become a standard tool for assessing discriminant validity in variance-based structural equation modeling (SEM), particularly in marketing and information systems research. However, its derivation and justification are explicitly tied to reflective measurement models. This limitation is problematic as composite models (e.g., Mode B, repeated indicators) are widely used in Partial Least Squares (PLS) path modeling, yet researchers lack a formally validated discriminant validity criterion for them.
proposals.hypothesis: The HTMT criterion can be formally adapted and its threshold values refined for composite measurement models, providing a statistically sound discriminant validity test that performs comparably to its reflective counterpart. This adaptation will not require the assumption of a common factor structure.
proposals.method: We will derive a new specification for the HTMT based on the composite model structure, building on Henseler's prior work on specifying composites in SEM. The methodological core involves Monte Carlo simulations to analyze the sampling distribution of the new composite-HTMT under various conditions (e.g., number of indicators, correlation levels, model misspecification). We will compare its performance against the traditional HTMT and the Fornell-Larcker criterion in composite settings. The analysis will use established PLS-SEM algorithms and statistical software (e.g., R, ADANCO) for simulation and empirical validation with real-world datasets involving composite constructs (e.g., brand equity indices, interaction terms).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, generalized HTMT criterion for composite models, filling a critical gap in the SEM toolbox. It will offer applied researchers in fields like marketing and design science a validated method for discriminant validity assessment when using composite constructs, enhancing the rigor of model evaluation. The work will also refine the theoretical understanding of discriminant validity in composite-based SEM.
proposals.novelty: While the HTMT is well-established, its extension to composite models is non-trivial and has not been formally proposed or validated. This directly challenges the implicit assumption that HTMT is only for reflective constructs, leveraging Henseler's expertise in composite specification to generalize a key methodological rule.

proposals.title: Extending the Heterotrait-Monotrait Ratio of Correlations (HTMT) to Models with Formative Measurement: A Monte Carlo Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: The Heterotrait-Monotrait ratio of correlations (HTMT) has become a widely adopted criterion for assessing discriminant validity in variance-based structural equation modeling (SEM) using reflective constructs. However, its applicability to models incorporating formative measurement remains unexplored and uncertain. Given the increasing use of formative constructs in fields like marketing and information systems, this gap poses a significant risk to the validity of empirical research, as researchers may be misapplying HTMT or lacking a suitable alternative.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the standard HTMT calculation, when applied to models with formative constructs, will yield systematically biased estimates of discriminant validity. Furthermore, we hypothesize that a modified HTMT specification, accounting for the formative measurement model's structure, can provide a valid and reliable criterion.
proposals.method: We will employ a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study, building on the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. The experimental design will manipulate key factors: number of indicators, indicator weights, inter-construct correlations, and sample size. We will compare the performance of the standard HTMT against a proposed refinement that uses the constructs' emergent scores (from the formative measurement model) in the correlation matrix. Analysis will focus on the bias, Type I, and Type II error rates of both approaches in detecting known population-level discriminant validity conditions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first rigorous evaluation and potential extension of HTMT for formative measurement models. It will deliver clear guidelines for researchers on whether and how to use HTMT in such contexts, thereby strengthening the methodological rigor of studies employing formative constructs. The outcome will be a refined specification, integrated into the composite modeling framework, for practical use.
proposals.novelty: While HTMT is well-established for reflective models, its behavior with formative constructs is entirely unexamined. This study moves beyond the reflective measurement paradigm to address a critical methodological gap in the assessment of discriminant validity for the broader class of composite models, directly extending Henseler's prior work on specification.

proposals.title: Extending the Heterotrait-Monotrait Ratio to Second-Order Constructs: A Specification and Validation Study
proposals.motivation: The Heterotrait-Monotrait (HTMT) ratio of correlations has become a standard criterion for assessing discriminant validity in variance-based structural equation modeling (SEM), particularly for first-order constructs. However, many advanced models in marketing, organizational research, and information systems employ second-order (formative or reflective) constructs to represent complex phenomena like brand equity or service quality. Pre-2026, there is no established, simulation-validated method to assess discriminant validity between such higher-order factors, creating a critical gap in the validation toolkit for composite models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an HTMT ratio, calculated using the latent variable scores of the second-order constructs derived from a consistent PLS-SEM estimation (e.g., the two-stage approach), provides a valid and reliable criterion for assessing discriminant validity among second-order factors, with a threshold value near 0.85 (for conceptually similar constructs) or 0.90 (for conceptually distinct ones) indicating sufficient discriminant validity.
proposals.method: The research will follow a design-science approach for method development. First, we will formally specify the algorithm for calculating HTMT for second-order constructs, building on the refined Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. This involves obtaining latent variable scores for the second-order constructs in the measurement model. We will then conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study in R to evaluate the performance (Type I and Type II error rates) of the proposed HTMT2 under various conditions (e.g., factor correlations, number of indicators, sample size). Finally, we will demonstrate the method's practical utility by applying it to an existing dataset involving second-order brand constructs, akin to the AI-disclosed brand voice research context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide researchers with a formally specified and empirically validated tool for rigorously testing discriminant validity in models with second-order constructs, enhancing the trustworthiness of their findings. It will extend the methodological rigor championed by Henseler's prior work on composite specification into the domain of higher-order model validation, directly serving the needs of applied researchers in fields like marketing and information systems.
proposals.novelty: While HTMT is well-established for first-order constructs, its adaptation and formal validation for second-order constructs is a novel and non-trivial extension, as it requires careful integration with the specific estimation procedures for higher-order composites in PLS-SEM, an area of Henseler's own methodological expertise.

proposals.title: Beyond Global Fit: A Composite-Based Framework for Assessing Partial Model Structures in PLS-SEM
proposals.motivation: The PLS-SEM community has long debated the need for a global goodness-of-fit (GoF) measure, with proponents arguing for its necessity for model validation and critics highlighting its conceptual mismatch with PLS's composite-based, variance-based nature. This debate remains unresolved, creating confusion for researchers and potentially leading to the misapplication of fit criteria derived from factor-based covariance modeling. A systematic investigation is needed to clarify whether a single global fit concept is appropriate or if alternative, partial assessment strategies are more coherent with PLS's design and objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for composite models estimated with PLS-SEM, a set of focused, partial fit indices assessing specific model features (e.g., composite reliability, collinearity, predictive relevance) provides more meaningful diagnostic information than a single global fit statistic. Furthermore, we posit that global fit measures can be misleading for composite models, especially when model misspecification is localized.
proposals.method: We will employ a Monte Carlo simulation study using the Henseler-Ogasawara specification for composites. We will generate data with controlled, localized misspecifications (e.g., in one structural path or one measurement model). We will then estimate models using PLS-SEM and calculate both traditional global fit approximations (like SRMR) and a proposed suite of partial indices. The analysis will compare the sensitivity and diagnostic accuracy of these different approaches in pinpointing the source of misfit. The simulation design will be informed by real-world model complexities from marketing and brand research contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, simulation-based argument for moving beyond the global fit debate in PLS-SEM. It will offer researchers a refined, composite-appropriate framework for model assessment, enhancing the rigor of PLS analyses.
